concepts.title: Nth order reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
${{\mathbf{N}}^{\mathbf{th }}}$ Order Reaction 

Let the reaction

$\text{A}\to \text{B}$

be ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }}}$ order reaction then

$\text{r}=\text{k}{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{n}}}$

where $\text{k}$ is constant.

$\text{r}=$ rate of reaction

$\frac{-\text{dA}}{\text{dt}}=\text{k}{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{n}}}$

$\int{\frac{-\text{dA}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{n}}}}}=\int{\text{kdt}}$

Let at $\text{t}=0,[~\text{A}]=\text{a}$

and $\text{t}=\text{t},[\text{A}]=\text{a}-\text{x}$

Thus ${{t}_{1/2}}\alpha \frac{1}{\left( {{a}^{n-1}} \right)}$

Let in two different conditions, initial concentration be ${{a}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }{{a}_{2}}$.

Then $\frac{{{\left( {{t}_{1/2}} \right)}_{1}}}{{{\left( {{t}_{1/2}} \right)}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{a}^{n-1}} \right)}_{2}}}{{{\left( {{a}^{n-1}} \right)}_{1}}}$

where subscript 2 & 1 stands for condition 1 & 2.

$\Rightarrow$ \(\frac{\left(t^1 / 2\right)_1}{\left(t^1 / 2\right)_2}=\frac{a_2^{n-1}}{\left(a_1^{n-1}\right)}\)

Thus through this equation we can know the half life time at given concentration if its half life time at some other concentration is given.

concepts.title: First order rate law
concepts.conceptBody:
First order rate law 

For reaction of the form$\text{A}\to $ products, a first order rate law takes the form

$\text{r}={{\text{k}}_{1\text{st}}}{{[\text{A}]}^{1}}$

writing the rate in the differential form this becomes

$\frac{\text{d}[\text{A}]}{\text{dt}}=-{{\text{k}}_{1\text{st}}}[\text{A}]$ … (7)

we need the minus sign as $\text{A}$ is a reactant and so its concentration decreases with time i.e the slope of a graph of [A] against time will be negative.

concepts.title: Hydrolys is of esters (CH3COOC2H5)
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydrolysis of esters $\left( \mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}\mathbf{COO}{{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{5}}} \right)$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{ ) (ester) }+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{HCl}\left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}$

The reaction rate is measured by titrating the acid $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)$ produced against a standard alkali solution. Note that when a test sample is prepared from the reacting mixture, there are two acids: one is mineral acid ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$($\text{HCl}$ or any other) and second is $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ produced. So volume of alkali used gives titration value for both acids. The data is collected in the following manner.

at $t=0.{{V}_{0}}$ is the volume $\text{NaOH}$ used to neutralise the mineral acid present $\left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)$being used as catalyst. (at $\text{t}=0$, no $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ is yet produced)

at $\text{t}=\infty $ (i.e. at the end of hydrolysis), ${{\text{V}}_{\infty }}$ is the volume of $\text{NaOH}$ used to neutralise whole of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ plus vol. of $\text{HCl}$ present. At $\text{t}=\infty $, volume $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ corresponds to volume of ester taken initially.

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{a}\propto {{\text{V}}_{\infty }}-{{\text{V}}_{0}}\left( \text{ as }{{\text{V}}_{0}}\equiv \text{ vol}\text{. of HCl} \right)$

At $\text{t}={{\text{t}}_{1}},{{\text{t}}_{2}},{{\text{t}}_{3}},\ldots .,{{\text{V}}_{1}},~{{\text{V}}_{2}},~{{\text{V}}_{3}},\ldots \ldots $ corresponds to vol. of HCl plus vol. of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ being produced

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{x}\propto {{\text{V}}_{\text{t}}}-{{\text{V}}_{0}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{a}-\text{x}\propto \left( {{\text{V}}_{\infty }}-{{\text{V}}_{0}} \right)-\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{t}}}-{{\text{V}}_{0}} \right)\quad $

$\text{kt}=2.303\log \frac{{{\text{V}}_{\infty }}-{{\text{V}}_{0}}}{~{{\text{V}}_{\infty }}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{t}}}}$

concepts.title: Gas pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Gas Pressure

The pressure, p, that a gas exerts is related to the volume $\text{V}$, the number of moles $\text{n}$, and the temperature, $\text{T}$, by the ideal gas equation.

$\text{pV}=\text{nRT}$

This can be rearranged to give

$\frac{\text{n}}{\text{V}}=\frac{\text{p}}{\text{RT}}$

the fraction $\text{n}/\text{V}$ is a measure of concentration; in SI it would be in moles ${{\text{m}}^{-3}}$. The relationship tells us that at constant temperature pressure is directly proportional to concentration.

In a reaction we will generally have a mixture of gases, and so we can talk about the partial pressure, $p$, of each gas $i$. The partial pressure of a gas is the pressure that the gas would exert if it occupied the volume on its own. Dalton’s Law states that the total pressure, ${{\text{P}}_{\text{T}}}$, is the sum of the partial pressures:

${{\text{P}}_{\text{T}}}={{\text{P}}_{1}}+{{\text{P}}_{2}}+{{\text{P}}_{3}}+\ldots .$

Each partial pressure follows an ideal gas law

$\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{i}}}}{\text{V}}=\frac{{{\text{p}}_{\text{i}}}}{\text{RT}}$

where ${{n}_{i}}$ is the number of moles of gas $i$. Therefore the partial pressure of species $i$ is a measure of its concentration.

In a reaction involving gases the only measurable pressure is the total pressure exerted by the system. However, to make kinetic measurements we need to know the concentration, that is the partial pressure, of the individual species. We need, therefore, to find some way of relating these two. An example of how this is done is considered here.

Consider the reaction between $\text{NO}$ and ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ in the gas phase

$2\text{NO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to 2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Overall three moves of gas go to two, so as the reaction proceeds there is a measurable decreases in the total pressure. The total pressure is the sum of the partial pressures:

${{\text{P}}_{\text{T}}}={{\text{P}}_{\text{NO}}}+{{\text{P}}_{\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}+{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$

There are three unknown quantities on the right, and only one measurable quantity, the total pressure; to solve this we are going to have to introduce some extra restrictions.

Suppose that at time zero there is no $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ present, and that the $\text{NO}$ and ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ are mixed in a 2: 1 ratio, just as the stoichiometry of the reaction. If the initial partial pressure of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is ${{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}},0}}$ then the initial partial pressure of $\text{NO}$ is $2{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}},0}}$ and so the initial total pressure, ${{\text{p}}_{0}}$, is $3{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}},0}}$. This is the situation set out on line 1 of the Table

Suppose after time t the partial pressure of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ has fallen by $\Delta {{\text{p}}_{\text{O}2}}$; due to the stoichiometry of the reaction the partial pressure of $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ will fall by $2\Delta {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$ and the partial pressure of $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ will increase by $2\Delta {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$. This is the situation set out on line 2 of the Table.

Our aim now is to find ${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$ in terms of measurable quantities. There are two steps. In going from line 2 to line 3 in the Table we have used the fact that ${{\text{p}}_{0}}=3{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}},0}}$ to substitute for ${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}},0}}$. From line 3 we then see that $\text{pT}={{\text{p}}_{0}}-\Delta {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$ from which it follows that $\Delta {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}={{\text{p}}_{0}}-\text{pT}$. This value of $\Delta {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$ is used in going from line 3 to 4.

So, finally, on line 4 we have an expression for ${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}$ in terms of measurable quantities:

${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}=\text{pT}-\frac{2}{3}{{\text{p}}_{0}}$

concepts.title: Comapring rate constants
concepts.conceptBody:
Comparing rate constants

As the half life of a first order reaction is independent of concentration it is easy simply to look at the rate constant and get an immediate feel for how fast it is. For example, the reaction

$2~{{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}\to 4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

has a first order rate constant of about $2000~{{\text{s}}^{-1}}$ at $400~\text{K}$; the half life is therefore $0.3~\text{ms}$, which makes the reaction quite fast.

On the other hand the second order reaction

$2\text{NOBr}\to 2\text{NO}+\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{ (gas phase) }$

has a rate constant at $280~\text{K}$ of $0.8~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}\text{d}{{\text{m}}^{3}}~{{\text{s}}^{-1}}$. Is this “faster” or “slower” than the first order reaction above? To answer this we need to know the concentration of the reactants. Suppose that the pressure of $\text{NOBr}$ is 0.1 atmospheres, corresponding to a concentration of $4.4\quad {{10}^{-3}}~\text{mold}{{\text{m}}^{-}}$ $^{3}$; using the above expression we find that the half life is $280~\text{s}-\text{so}$, under these conditions, the reaction is much slower than the decomposition of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$.

The key point to understand here is that we cannot compare directly the rates of reactions of different orders by comparing their rate constants; concentration has to be taken into account.

concepts.title: Radioactivity
concepts.conceptBody:
Radioactivity

All radioactive decay follows first order kinetics. The only difference from chemical reactions is that temperature does not influence the rate of radioactive decay.

In radioactive decay the term used for identifying the reaction rate is the number of nuclei rather than concentration.

$\text{A}\to \text{B}$

$d\text{ }\frac{-\text{d}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{dt}}=\lambda {{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$ … (a)

$\lambda $ is similar to $\text{k}$ in chemical reactions and is known as decay or disintegration constant and ${{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$ is the number of nuclei of $\text{A}$ at a given instant of time.

$\ln \frac{{{\text{N}}_{0}}}{~{{\text{N}}_{\text{t}}}}=\lambda \text{t};\quad {{\text{N}}_{\text{t}}}={{\text{N}}_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\lambda \text{t}}}$ … (b)

${{\text{t}}_{1/2}}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda }$

A term called average life time is introduced here which is given by $1/\lambda $.

$\therefore \quad {{\text{t}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda }$

Average lifetime means the life time of a single isolated nuclei. If ${{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}=1$, and this nuclei decays in one second, then according to the equation (11a), $\frac{-\text{d}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{dt}}=\lambda $

If a nucleus of A decays after 2 seconds, then

$\frac{-\text{d}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{dt}}=\frac{1}{2}=\lambda $. The average lifetime of the nucleus is therefore $1/\lambda $.

${{\text{t}}_{\text{aV}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda }=\frac{{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}}{0.693}=1.44{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}$

Activity of a radioactive element is the rate at which it decays and is given by $\lambda N$.

$\text{A}=\lambda \text{N}$

Since according to equation \[\left( 11b \right)\]

${{N}_{\text{t}}}={{\text{N}}_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\lambda \text{t}}}$

Multiplying by $\lambda $, we get

$\lambda {{N}_{t}}=\lambda {{N}_{0}}{{e}^{-\lambda t}}$

${{A}_{t}}={{A}_{0}}{{e}^{-\lambda t}},$

$\frac{-\text{d}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{dt}}=\text{q}-\text{A}=\text{q}-\lambda \text{N}\left( \frac{-\text{d}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{dt}}\text{ and A will not be equal } \right)$

Specific activity is the activity of a unit mass of a radioactive substance.

Units of radioactivity 

1 curie $=3.7\quad {{10}^{10}}$ disintegrations per second $(\text{dps})=3.7\quad {{10}^{10}}~\text{Bq}$.

1 Becquerel = 1 disintegrations per second $(\text{dps})$

concepts.title: Amount of radioactive substance left after n half-lives
concepts.conceptBody:
Amount of radioactive substance left after $n$ half-lives: 
Let us start with the amount of radioactive substance in the beginning as ${{\text{A}}_{0}}$.
Then, amount of radioactive substance after first half-life $=\frac{{{A}_{0}}}{{{2}^{1}}}$
Amount of radioactive substance after second half-life $=\frac{{{\text{A}}_{0}}}{{{2}^{2}}}$
And amount of radioactive substance after $n$ half-life $=\frac{{{A}_{0}}}{{{2}^{n}}}$
